personality_voice.book_baseline: Slughorn is defined by a cluster of tightly interlocked traits: vanity, social calculation, comfort-seeking, cowardice dressed as pragmatism, genuine warmth toward talented students, and a collector's mentality toward people. His voice is expansive, self-congratulatory, and name-dropping ('Gwenog Jones, captain of the Holyhead Harpies — free tickets whenever I want them!'). He is capable of genuine emotion — his grief over Lily is real — but he habitually retreats behind bluster and self-interest. He is not cruel; his flaw is moral cowardice and self-preservation, not malice. He speaks in long, digressive sentences, frequently pivoting from sentiment to self-interest mid-thought. He is also capable of sharp, unguarded moments: his terror when Harry asks about Horcruxes ('WRONG!' bellowed at full volume), his half-proud, half-irritable admission about the Intruder Charm, his unselfconscious collector's language about the Black brothers ('I'd have liked the set'). His prejudice about Muggle-borns is present but unconscious — he genuinely believes he is not prejudiced while demonstrating it.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the core personality well: the walrus mustache, the belly, the name-dropping photographs, the crystallized pineapple, the social maneuvering, the warmth toward Harry, the cowardice about Voldemort. His voice retains the expansive, self-amused quality. The film adds a small invented anecdote about a fish named Francis (a surrogate for Lily), which is tonally consistent with the character. His terror at the Horcrux question is preserved. His collector's vanity is shown through the shelf of photographs and his comments about students. His cowardice is shown in his initial refusal to return to Hogwarts and his reluctance to give the memory.
personality_voice.difference: Several secondary traits are compressed or lost. The film removes his sharp, unselfconscious social cruelty — the way he simply ignores Ron at every encounter (present in the film but less systematically developed), the cold dismissal of Belby when his uncle connection proves thin, the calculated way he works the Slug Club train compartment. His unconscious Muggle-born prejudice ('Funny how that sometimes happens') is present in the film but Harry's cold pushback ('Not really') is absent, softening the exchange. His half-proud, half-irritable self-awareness ('Two. Didn't hear my Intruder Charm go off, I was taking a bath') is compressed in the film into simpler self-deprecation. His voice in the film is slightly more genial and less barbed than in the books.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The main personality traits — vanity, social calculation, cowardice, genuine warmth, collector mentality — are preserved and recognizable. The loss is in secondary texture: the sharper edges of his social cruelty, the unconscious prejudice being called out, the more complex self-awareness. This warrants a minor deduction but not a heavy one, as the dominant pattern is intact.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Slughorn serves three structural functions: (1) gatekeeper of the Horcrux memory, the most plot-critical piece of information in HBP; (2) Potions teacher whose class drives the Half-Blood Prince subplot; (3) social operator whose Slug Club illuminates the wizarding world's class dynamics and Harry's celebrity status. His agency is substantial: he actively resists Dumbledore, actively avoids Harry after the first Horcrux attempt, stops issuing party invitations as a defensive measure, and ultimately makes the pivotal decision to surrender the memory. He is also the one who introduces Felix Felicis as a prize, which drives a key plot thread.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The film preserves all three structural functions. Slughorn remains the gatekeeper of the memory, the Potions teacher, and the social operator. His active resistance and avoidance are shown. His decision to surrender the memory is the emotional climax of his arc. Felix Felicis is introduced by him. The Slug Club dinners are shown. His agency in the memory scene is intact — he makes the choice himself after Harry's appeal.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The film compresses his active avoidance after the first failed attempt. In the books, Slughorn stops issuing party invitations entirely as a calculated defensive move, Harry lags behind after multiple Potions lessons and is repeatedly evaded, and there is a sustained cat-and-mouse dynamic over weeks. In the film this compression means his agency as an active resister is less visible. The train compartment Slug Club scene — where he actively works the room, interrogates students about their connections, and demonstrates his social machinery in detail — is replaced by a simpler dinner party. The bezoar classroom scene is present but the sustained pattern of Slughorn raving about Harry's abilities over multiple lessons is compressed to a single class.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The core narrative functions and the key act of agency (surrendering the memory) are preserved. The compression of his active avoidance and the reduction of the Slug Club's social machinery reduce the visibility of his agency as a resister and operator, but do not eliminate it. A moderate deduction is warranted.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Slughorn's motivations are layered and consistent: (1) self-preservation above all — he hid for a year, he fears Voldemort, he fears being implicated; (2) genuine affection for talented students, especially Lily, which creates real guilt about the Horcrux conversation; (3) vanity and the desire to be associated with greatness, which makes Harry irresistible to him; (4) moral cowardice — he knows what he did was wrong ('I am not proud... I think I may have done great damage that day') but cannot bring himself to act without being pushed. The internal conflict is between his self-interest and his genuine shame and affection. He is not evil; he is weak.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film preserves all four motivational layers. His self-preservation is shown in his hiding, his fear of Voldemort's name, his resistance to returning. His affection for Lily is made more explicit through the Francis fish anecdote. His vanity is shown through the shelf and his treatment of Harry. His shame is shown in his trembling hands and 'Don't think too badly of me once you've seen it.' The internal conflict between self-interest and guilt is the engine of the Hagrid's hut scene and is well-rendered.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The film simplifies the motivation slightly by making the Lily connection more sentimentalized (the fish anecdote) and less complex. In the books, Slughorn's guilt is more specifically about having given Riddle information that enabled mass murder; the film preserves this but the weight of it is slightly reduced by the more emotional framing. His motivation to avoid Harry after the first attempt — calculated self-protection, not just embarrassment — is less clearly shown.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Motivations are simplified but correct. The core conflict between self-interest and guilt/affection is intact. The simplification does not change the character's fundamental motivation structure.

character_arc.book_baseline: Slughorn's arc in HBP runs from hiding/self-preservation → reluctant return → active avoidance of Harry → gradual softening through social contact → crisis of conscience at Hagrid's hut → surrender of the memory as an act of moral courage. The arc is not a transformation into a hero; it is a small, hard-won act of moral courage from a fundamentally cowardly man. The endpoint — 'I am not proud... I think I may have done great damage that day' — is the culmination. In DH, he returns to fight at Hogwarts, dueling Voldemort alongside McGonagall and Kingsley, which represents the completion of his arc from cowardice to action.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film preserves the arc through HBP faithfully: hiding → return → avoidance → surrender of memory. The Hagrid's hut scene is the emotional climax and is well-executed. The DH arc (returning to fight) is present in the film corpus (he is shown arriving with reinforcements and dueling Voldemort alongside McGonagall and Kingsley).
character_arc.difference: The middle section of the arc — the sustained cat-and-mouse over weeks, the multiple failed attempts, the growing desperation — is compressed. This means the surrender of the memory feels slightly less hard-won in the film, because the audience has seen fewer failed attempts. The arc's shape is correct but some of its texture is lost through compression.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc is compressed but faithful. The key turning points (return, avoidance, surrender, fighting) are all present. The compression reduces the sense of how difficult the surrender was, but does not change the arc's direction or meaning.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Three key relationships: (1) Slughorn/Harry — a complex dynamic of mutual use: Slughorn wants to collect Harry, Harry wants the memory; genuine warmth develops but the power dynamic is always present. (2) Slughorn/Lily — the emotional core of his guilt; she is his favorite student and her death is the wound he cannot face. (3) Slughorn/Dumbledore — a relationship of mutual respect and mild antagonism; Dumbledore knows exactly how to handle Slughorn and uses Harry as a tool to do so. (4) Slughorn/Voldemort (Riddle) — the relationship that defines his guilt; he was charmed by Riddle and gave him dangerous information.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: All four relationships are present. The Slughorn/Harry dynamic is well-rendered, including the mutual use and the genuine warmth. The Slughorn/Lily relationship is made more explicit and emotional through the fish anecdote. The Slughorn/Dumbledore dynamic is present in the opening scene. The Slughorn/Riddle relationship is shown in the Pensieve memory.
key_relationships.difference: The Slughorn/Harry relationship loses some of its social-calculation dimension — in the books, Slughorn's pursuit of Harry through the Slug Club is more systematically shown as a collector's project, with Harry as the 'jewel of his collection.' The film shows this but less elaborately. The Slughorn/Dumbledore relationship is compressed to the opening scene and loses the ongoing dynamic of Dumbledore managing Slughorn from a distance. Ron's systematic exclusion from Slughorn's attention — which defines the Slughorn/Ron non-relationship and illuminates Slughorn's class snobbery — is present in the film but less developed.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The key relationships are mostly faithful with some compression. The emotional dynamics are preserved. The loss is in the elaboration of the collector/trophy dynamic with Harry and the ongoing Dumbledore management dynamic.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Slughorn's complexity rests on several elements: (1) his unconscious class prejudice (ignoring Ron, the Muggle-born 'funny how that sometimes happens' exchange); (2) his collector's mentality shown in granular detail across multiple Slug Club scenes; (3) his active, calculated avoidance of Harry over weeks — showing he is not merely passive but strategically self-protective; (4) his genuine pedagogical enthusiasm (the bezoar scene, his delight at Harry's potions work) coexisting with his vanity; (5) the moral weight of his guilt — he knows he may have enabled Voldemort's worst acts; (6) his small acts of self-interest even in emotional moments (pocketing the acromantula venom during Aragog's eulogy).
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves (4), (5), and (6) well. The acromantula venom extraction is shown. His pedagogical enthusiasm is shown. His guilt is shown. The fish anecdote adds emotional texture not in the books but consistent with the character. However, (1) is reduced — the Muggle-born prejudice exchange is softened and Harry's cold response is absent. (2) is compressed — the Slug Club machinery is shown in one dinner party rather than across multiple scenes. (3) is significantly compressed — the weeks of active avoidance, the stopped party invitations, the repeated evasions after Potions class, are reduced to a brief reference.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The most significant loss is the sustained cat-and-mouse dynamic that shows Slughorn as an active, calculating self-protector rather than merely a passive coward. In the books, his avoidance of Harry after the first Horcrux attempt is itself an act of agency — he stops the parties, he leaves the dungeon fast, he doesn't answer his door. This shows a more complex, strategically self-aware character. The film's compression makes him seem more passively reluctant than actively evasive. The Muggle-born prejudice nuance is also reduced.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: Noticeable simplification of the active-avoidance dimension and the social-machinery dimension. The character remains substantially recognizable and the most important nuances (guilt, warmth, cowardice, collector mentality) are preserved. But the loss of the sustained evasion pattern and the softening of the class prejudice texture represent a meaningful reduction in complexity.

lost_or_transferred_material: Harry's cold response to Slughorn's 'funny how that sometimes happens' comment about Muggle-borns ('Not really') — removed, softening Slughorn's unconscious prejudice

The sustained multi-week cat-and-mouse dynamic after the first failed Horcrux attempt — Slughorn stopping party invitations, evading Harry after Potions, not answering his door — compressed to near-absence

The train compartment Slug Club scene with its detailed social machinery (interrogating Zabini, Belby, McLaggen, Neville about their connections; the cold dismissal of Belby when his uncle connection proves thin) — replaced by a simpler dinner party
Slughorn's half-proud, half-irritable self-awareness about his own Intruder Charm ('Two. Didn't hear it go off, I was taking a bath') — compressed
The collector's language about the Black brothers ('I'd have liked the set') — present in film but less developed

Dumbledore's explicit post-visit analysis of Slughorn's psychology to Harry ('He likes his comfort... he prefers the backseat') — absent from film, reducing the audience's analytical framework for the character
Slughorn's comment about Amelia Bones's death shaking him — absent, reducing the specificity of his fear
The bezoar classroom scene's full dynamic — Slughorn's sustained raving about Harry over multiple lessons, Hermione's growing frustration — compressed to a single class
score_caps_applied: No hard caps triggered. Total of 78 falls within acceptable range given the level of compression and nuance loss identified.
comparative.key_observations: The film adaptation of Slughorn is substantially faithful. The core personality — vain, warmly calculating, cowardly, genuinely fond of talented students, haunted by guilt over Lily and the Horcrux conversation — is preserved and well-performed. The key narrative function (gatekeeper of the Horcrux memory) and the emotional arc (cowardice to a small act of moral courage) are intact. The Hagrid's hut memory scene is the emotional centerpiece of both versions and is faithfully adapted, with the fish/Lily anecdote serving as an effective film-original surrogate for the book's more extended emotional manipulation. The main losses are in texture and complexity: the sustained active-avoidance dynamic that shows Slughorn as a strategic self-protector rather than merely a passive coward; the granular social machinery of the Slug Club across multiple scenes; and the softening of his unconscious Muggle-born prejudice. These losses reduce the character's complexity without fundamentally altering who he is. The score of 78 reflects a faithful but compressed adaptation that preserves the essential character while losing some of the book's richer behavioral texture.
